nodes.text: Good evening. The day is behind you now. Let's take a moment to see how you moved through it.
If you had to describe the 'weather' of your workday today, what would it be?

options.text: Clear and Sunny (Productive)
Stormy (Challenging)
Foggy (Uncertain/Slow)
Changing Winds (Mixed)

nodes.text: You felt today was {A1_OPEN.text}. When things were moving well, what role did you play in that momentum?

options.text: I set a clear intention and followed through
I reacted quickly to opportunities
I mostly stayed out of the way of a good flow
It felt like luck or good timing

nodes.text: Even in a good flow, there's a choice. Did you push for more, or savor the ease?

options.text: I pushed to maximize the output
I savored the ease and stayed steady
I worried it wouldn't last

nodes.text: Today felt {A1_OPEN.text}. When the 'weather' turned, where did your focus go first?

options.text: To what I could immediately fix or change
To the external factors causing the friction
To how this was affecting my personal plans
I looked for who was responsible

nodes.text: In that moment of friction, did you feel like a participant or a spectator?

options.text: A participant trying to navigate
A spectator watching it happen
A target of the circumstances

nodes.text: Agency isn't about controlling the weather; it's about how you pilot the ship. You noticed {A1_Q_AGENCY_HIGH.value || A1_Q_AGENCY_LOW.value} as your primary mode. That's your locus of control in action.
Now, let's shift from how you navigated the day to what you actually put into it.
Think of your energy today as a currency. How did you spend most of it?

options.text: Investing in others (helping, teaching, supporting)
Securing my own outputs (focusing strictly on my tasks)
Protecting myself from interruptions/demands
Waiting for the resources I was promised

nodes.text: Was there a moment today where you felt you deserved more than what you received (credit, time, help)?

options.text: Yes, I felt my contribution wasn't fully recognized
A little, but I moved past it
No, I was focused on the work itself
I felt I gave less than I could have

nodes.text: When you think about your team's success today, how much of it felt like 'your' win versus 'their' responsibility?

options.text: I felt responsible for helping everyone win
I did my part; the rest was on them
I felt they owed me more support

nodes.text: You operated from a place of contribution today. When we focus on what we give, we build 'Organizational Citizenship'—the invisible glue that makes teams work.
It's natural to feel like the scale is unbalanced sometimes. Entitlement is often just a shield for feeling undervalued. Recognizing it is the first step toward reclaiming your power to give.
Finally, let's look at the 'radius' of your concern. How wide was your world today?
When you encountered a challenge today, who was in the frame of your thoughts?

options.text: Mostly me—how it affected my stress and my goals
Me and my immediate teammates
The end user or the mission of the company
Someone else who was struggling more than me

nodes.text: If you could change one person's experience of today (besides your own), who would it be?

options.text: A colleague who seemed overwhelmed
A customer who didn't get what they needed
My manager, to make their job easier
I didn't really think about others' experiences today

nodes.text: When you think about the impact of your work today, where does the ripple end?

options.text: With my own completion of tasks
With my team's progress
With the person who eventually uses what I built

nodes.text: You've practiced 'Self-Transcendence' today. By looking beyond yourself, you contextalize your own challenges and find a deeper sense of purpose.
A narrow radius is often a survival mechanism for a high-stress day. But even a small shift outward—noticing a teammate's effort—can break the cycle of self-centered stress.
Reflection Complete. Today, you navigated with {axis1.dominant} agency, showed a {axis2.dominant} orientation, and maintained a {axis3.dominant} radius of concern.
Rest well. Tomorrow is a new day to choose how you show up.
